article_title: NASA Moves to Decouple Blue Origin's Moon Landers From New Glenn After Pad Explosion, Eyeing a New Launcher for Blue Moon
summary: Fully-verified follow-up on NASA decoupling Blue Moon from New Glenn; every Isaacman/Limp quote verbatim. The sole automated warning is a false positive (the flagged word is inside a verbatim source quote).

findings.message: Loaded-language pattern matched inside a verbatim quote (false positive)
findings.details: The automated detector flagged 'obviously'. It appears only inside a direct, verified quote from NASA Administrator Jared Isaacman ('...you've got SpaceX and Blue Origin, and obviously one of them is down a pad right now'), not in the bot's own prose. No corrective action required.

content_preview.title: NASA Moves to Decouple Blue Origin's Moon Landers From New Glenn After Pad Explosion, Eyeing a New Launcher for Blue Moon
content_preview.summary: After the May 28 New Glenn pad loss, NASA Administrator Jared Isaacman says the agency is decoupling the Blue Moon landers from the rocket and would like them flown on a different launcher to protect Artemis deadlines.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

NASA wants Blue Origin's robotic Blue Moon Mark 1 cargo lander, and potentially its Mark 2 crewed lander, flown to space on a rocket other than New Glenn following the May 28 explosion that damaged the company's only orbital launch pad. An agency spokesperson confirmed to [Spaceflight Now](https://spaceflightnow.com/2026/06/04/nasa-head-urges-new-launcher-for-blue-origins-moon-landers-to-meet-artemis-mission-deadlines/) that NASA would like to see the launches of the Blue Moon Mark ...

editor_notes.content_quality: Comprehensive, well-structured News piece (1038 words) on NASA's decision to decouple the Blue Moon landers from New Glenn, balancing the agency's position, Blue Origin's recovery plan, and the structural fairing constraint.
editor_notes.source_verification: All 4 snapshots read from disk (HTTP 200). source-0 (Spaceflight Now, June 4) confirms verbatim the Isaacman 'de-coupling the lander…' quote, the 'laser focused…before 2028…Artemis 3…2028' quote, the 'real heavy lift…obviously one of them is down a pad' quote, the seven-meter / 5.2-meter fairing figures, and the FOX Business interview attribution. source-2 (Spaceflight Now, June 3) confirms Limp's propellant-farm quote (the article correctly drops SFN's editorial '[cryogenic methane]' bracket), the water-tower / repaired-in-place quotes, the 'We will fly again before the end of this year. Gradatim Ferociter' pledge, and Isaacman's 'responded incredibly quickly' quote. source-1 (SpaceNews) confirms the vertical-assembly approach, the seven-month repair estimate, the SpaceX 15-month SLC-40 (2016) and Antares/Wallops (October 2014) comparisons, the May 26 lunar-rover selection, and the Artemis 3 Orion/Starship docking. source-3 (Astronomy) confirms May 28, 274-foot, seven main engines, LOX/LNG, the two lightning-protection towers (the article corrects the source's 'lighting' typo and expands 'SLC-36' to 'Launch Complex 36'), and the Mark 1 / Mark 2 roles.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: Every quote is verbatim and correctly attributed; every specific (dates, dimensions, repair timelines, historical pad-repair comparisons) traces to a source. Both internal cross-references (the June 2 New Glenn explosion article and the May 27 Moon Base contracts article) were verified to exist. The CNBC source was bot-blocked and correctly excluded.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional. The automated loaded-language flag ('obviously') is a false positive — the word occurs only inside a verbatim Isaacman quote.
editor_notes.originality: Legitimate force-follow-up to the June 2 explosion coverage — centers on the new June 4 decoupling development and cross-references the prior explosion and Moon-contract articles.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: High-quality, fully-verified follow-up. APPROVE — the single automated warning is a verified false positive.
